title: Recursion
description: Master C# recursion patterns: base cases, Dictionary memoization, backtracking, and iterative conversion for coding interviews.

sections.title: Recursion and Base Cases
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs: (1) a base case that stops recursion, (2) a recursive case that moves toward the base case. Think: what's the simplest input? That's your base case. Common pattern: `if (n <= 1) return result; return f(n-1)`. Always ensure the recursive call reduces the problem size. Stack depth limit is ~1000-10000 in C# depending on stack size.

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive method `int Power(int baseVal, int exp)` that computes baseVal^exp. Base case: exp == 0 returns 1. Recursive case: baseVal * Power(baseVal, exp - 1). Print Power(2, 10) and Power(3, 4).

validation.message: Use base case exp == 0 and recursive case baseVal * Power(baseVal, exp - 1).

exercise.hint: if (exp == 0) return 1; return baseVal * Power(baseVal, exp - 1);

sections.title: Memoization with Dictionary
sections.explanation: Memoization caches results of expensive recursive calls. Use a `Dictionary<TKey, TResult>` as the cache. Before computing, check if the result exists in the dictionary. After computing, store it. This transforms exponential-time recursion (like naive Fibonacci O(2^n)) into O(n). Pass the dictionary as a parameter or use a static field.

exercise.prompt: Write a memoized recursive method `int ClimbStairs(int n, Dictionary<int, int> memo)` that returns the number of ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time. Base cases: n <= 1 returns 1. Print results for n = 5, 10, and 20.

validation.message: Use Dictionary for memoization, check before computing, store after computing.

exercise.hint: if (n <= 1) return 1; Check memo, compute ClimbStairs(n-1) + ClimbStairs(n-2), store in memo.

sections.title: Backtracking Pattern
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores all candidates by building solutions incrementally and abandoning paths that can't lead to valid solutions. Template: (1) if solution complete, save it. (2) for each choice: make the choice, recurse, undo the choice. The "undo" step is critical—it restores state for the next iteration. Used for permutations, combinations, subsets, Sudoku, N-Queens.

exercise.prompt: Use backtracking to generate all combinations of {1,2,3,4} of size 2. Print each combination on its own line as comma-separated values. Print the total count at the end. Should be 6 combinations.

validation.message: 6 combinations of size 2 from {1,2,3,4}, then count 6.
Copy current when saving, add candidate, recurse, then remove (backtrack).

exercise.hint: if (current.Count == k) add copy to result. Loop from start to n: add i, recurse with i+1, remove last.

sections.title: Converting Recursion to Iteration
sections.explanation: Recursive solutions risk stack overflow for large inputs. Convert to iterative using: (1) a Stack to simulate the call stack, (2) bottom-up dynamic programming with an array. Fibonacci: instead of top-down recursion, build from `dp[0]` up. This uses O(n) time and O(1) space with rolling variables. Interviewers often ask for both recursive and iterative solutions.

exercise.prompt: Convert the recursive ClimbStairs to an iterative bottom-up approach using O(1) space (just two variables). Print results for n = 5, 10, and 20.

validation.message: Use iterative loop with two rolling variables for O(1) space.

exercise.hint: int a = 1, b = 1; for i from 2 to n: int next = a + b; a = b; b = next; return b.
